text: The CT scan shows a two point five centimeters adenocarcinoma of the body of the pancreas. The red line is the classical plane of left splenopancreatectomy and the green one RAMPS plane. In orange the tissue on the posterior plane gained with RAMPS technique. According to our techniques, we use five trochers. The patient is positioned in the French position with the legs spread and the surgeon in between the legs.

The surgeon suspends the hepatic artery on a loop and creates a retropancreatic tunnel, suspending the neck of the pancreas on another loop. A formal lymphadenectomy is performed, removing lymph nodes, lymphovascular, and neural tissue from the periventricial plane. The arteries are skeletonized in a 360-degree manner. The left gastric vein is clipped and divided, while the left gastric artery is suspended on a loop. The splenic artery is identified and suspended on a loop according to Rapp's technique.
The optic camera is repositioned from the middle to the left side of the patient, who is positioned with the head down to provide a clear view of the left border of the superior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon continues dissection on the periventric plane to remove the retroportal lamina, frequently invaded by the tumor. The neck of the pancreas is sectioned using a stapler, followed by suspension and division of the splenic vein with a vascular stapler. The anterior border of the superior mesenteric vein is exposed, allowing further removal of the retroportal lamina up to the aorta and left border of the celiac trunk. The inferior mesenteric vein is controlled and divided to optimize lateral and posterior margins for complete dissection around the select trunk.
The surgeon controls and divides the splenic artery, followed by completing the lymphadenectomy around the celiac trunk.

The surgeon removes the lymph nodes from the right side of the celiac trunk. The splenopancreatic block is mobilized behind the gelatofascia, which is identified at the superior pole of the kidney. The adrenal gland is spared during the anterior RAMPS procedure.
The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels and removes the specimen through a fan steel incision. The surgical field is exposed, revealing the superior mesenteric artery and the left kidney vein.
